narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 46% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your profile: a Year 1 decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 3, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 25.5, undergraduate GPA of 3.4, identifying as female and Black, and planning to attend part-time. Compared to typical students—who average higher Year 1 and Year 3 deciles around 5.7 and 5.5, an LSAT around 37, and mostly full-time enrollment—your metrics show some strengths like your solid undergrad GPA above the average of 3.21, but notable gaps elsewhere. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure since it's lower than what's typical for successful students. Next in influence was your LSAT score, also contributing toward failure risk because it's well below the norm. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, similarly signaling lower performance relative to peers and nudging the odds against passing. Your racial background as Black had a moderate push in the same direction, given its underrepresentation among bar passers. Family income in the middle bracket and part-time status each played smaller roles toward higher failure risk, while your gender had the least impact among these factors. Overall, these elements combined to outweigh your strong undergrad GPA, which tried but couldn't fully counterbalance the rest. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can realistically change: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that critical score closer to or above the average, and if reapplying, aim for stronger law school grades in early years through dedicated study habits. Building a full-time study schedule could also help align with the profiles of those who pass. With targeted effort on these, you could shift the prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA strength that proves your potential.
